text: Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy. It takes place mainly in the chloroplasts of plant cells, which contain the green pigment chlorophyll. During the light-dependent reactions, chlorophyll absorbs sunlight and splits water molecules, releasing oxygen as a byproduct. The energy captured is stored temporarily in ATP and NADPH. In the Calvin cycle, also called the light-independent reactions, this stored energy is used to fix carbon dioxide from the air into glucose. The overall equation is six carbon dioxide plus six water yields one glucose plus six oxygen. Photosynthesis is the foundation of most food chains and is responsible for the oxygen in Earth's atmosphere.

The Roman Empire was one of the largest and most influential civilizations in world history. It began in 27 BC when Augustus became the first emperor, following the collapse of the Roman Republic. At its height under Trajan around 117 AD, the empire stretched from Britain in the northwest to the Persian Gulf in the southeast. Roman engineering produced roads, aqueducts, and concrete structures that still stand today. The empire eventually split into western and eastern halves. The Western Roman Empire fell in 476 AD when the Germanic leader Odoacer deposed the last emperor, while the Eastern or Byzantine Empire endured until 1453.

A black hole is a region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape from it. Black holes form when massive stars collapse at the end of their life cycles. The boundary beyond which escape is impossible is called the event horizon. At the center lies the singularity, a point of infinite density. The first image of a black hole, at the center of galaxy M87, was captured by the Event Horizon Telescope in 2019. Supermassive black holes, millions to billions of times the mass of the Sun, sit at the centers of most galaxies including our own Milky Way.

The human heart is a muscular organ that pumps blood throughout the body via the circulatory system. It has four chambers: two atria on top and two ventricles below. Deoxygenated blood enters the right atrium, moves to the right ventricle, and is pumped to the lungs to pick up oxygen. Oxygenated blood returns to the left atrium, flows into the left ventricle, and is pumped out through the aorta to the rest of the body. A healthy adult heart beats about 60 to 100 times per minute at rest, pumping roughly five litres of blood every minute.

Machine learning is a branch of artificial intelligence in which systems learn patterns from data rather than following explicitly programmed rules. Supervised learning trains a model on labeled examples to predict outcomes for new inputs. Unsupervised learning finds structure in unlabeled data, such as clustering similar items. Reinforcement learning trains an agent to take actions that maximize a reward signal. Common models include linear regression, decision trees, support vector machines, and neural networks. Overfitting, where a model memorizes training data instead of generalizing, is a central challenge addressed by techniques like cross-validation and regularization.

A volcano is a rupture in the crust of a planet that allows molten rock, ash, and gases to escape from a magma chamber below the surface. Volcanoes are often found where tectonic plates diverge or converge. When magma reaches the surface it is called lava. The explosiveness of an eruption depends largely on the viscosity of the magma and its dissolved gas content. The Volcanic Explosivity Index rates eruptions on a scale from zero to eight. Mount Vesuvius famously destroyed the Roman city of Pompeii in 79 AD, burying it under ash and preserving it for centuries.

The Internet is a global network of interconnected computers that communicate using standardized protocols, chiefly TCP/IP. Its origins lie in ARPANET, a United States research network from the late 1960s. Data is broken into packets that are routed independently across the network and reassembled at the destination. The World Wide Web, invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989, is a service running on top of the Internet that links documents through hyperlinks. Today the Internet underpins commerce, communication, and the delivery of cloud services to billions of users worldwide.

Antibiotics are medicines that fight bacterial infections by killing bacteria or preventing them from reproducing. Penicillin, discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928, was the first widely used antibiotic and transformed modern medicine. Different antibiotics target different bacterial structures, such as the cell wall or protein synthesis machinery. Crucially, antibiotics do not work against viruses like the common cold or influenza. Overuse and misuse have driven the rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria, a major public health threat, because resistant strains survive treatment and multiply.

Weather refers to short-term atmospheric conditions such as temperature, humidity, and precipitation on a given day, while climate describes the average weather patterns of a region over decades. The greenhouse effect occurs when gases like carbon dioxide and methane trap heat in the atmosphere, warming the planet. Human activity, especially burning fossil fuels, has increased greenhouse gas concentrations and driven global warming. Consequences include rising sea levels, more frequent extreme weather events, and shifts in ecosystems. The distinction matters: a cold day is weather, but a decades-long warming trend is climate.

DNA, or deoxyribonucleic acid, is the molecule that carries the genetic instructions for the development and functioning of all known living organisms. It has a double-helix structure, discovered by Watson and Crick in 1953 using data from Rosalind Franklin. DNA is made of four bases: adenine, thymine, guanine, and cytosine, which pair specifically, A with T and G with C. Sequences of these bases form genes, which encode proteins. During cell division, DNA is replicated so each new cell receives a complete copy of the genetic code.

Chlorophyll is a family of green pigments found in cyanobacteria and the chloroplasts of algae and plants. There are several types, chlorophyll a and b being the most common in plants. The pigments absorb light most strongly in the blue and red parts of the spectrum and reflect green, which is why leaves appear green. Chlorophyll is essential but is only one component of the broader light-harvesting machinery.

Byzantine architecture flourished in the Eastern Roman Empire, characterized by large domes, rounded arches, and elaborate mosaics. The Hagia Sophia in Constantinople, completed in 537 AD, is its most celebrated example. This style influenced religious buildings across Eastern Europe and the Mediterranean for centuries.

A neutron star is the collapsed core of a massive supergiant star, extremely dense but not dense enough to form a black hole. A teaspoon of neutron-star material would weigh billions of tonnes. Rapidly rotating neutron stars that emit beams of radiation are observed as pulsars.
